theme.en: AI Demand Tightens Every Layer of Supply

dek.en: TSMC warns that accelerator demand will remain ahead of supply while SK hynix prepares a major DRAM expansion. The pressure is spreading from foundry and memory into optical links, materials, and export policy.

title.en: Foundry & Manufacturing
TSMC says fully meeting AI-chip demand remains a long way off

summary.en: TSMC's chief executive says it will take a long time for the foundry to fully satisfy demand for AI chips. The warning indicates that leading-edge wafer capacity and advanced packaging will remain constraints even as TSMC continues expanding its manufacturing footprint.

title.en: TSMC is reportedly set to manufacture Tesla's next FSD chip

summary.en: TSMC is reportedly lined up to manufacture Tesla's next-generation Full Self-Driving hardware. A production commitment would add another large custom-accelerator program to TSMC's AI and automotive pipeline, increasing competition for advanced-node capacity.

title.en: Memory
SK hynix roadmap puts Yongin at 360,000 DRAM wafers a month

summary.en: SK hynix's disclosed capacity roadmap assigns its Yongin cluster an eventual 360,000 DRAM wafer starts per month. The scale shows how aggressively the company is preparing for sustained HBM and server-memory demand, although wafer starts do not translate directly into finished-chip output.

title.en: Lexar manager expects RAM prices to double by year-end

summary.en: A Lexar regional manager says RAM prices could double by the end of 2026 as supply remains tight for another eight to nine months. Current discounts may reflect distributors clearing older inventory or moving stock across regions rather than a durable easing in DRAM pricing.

title.en: AMD EXPO ULL adds memory subtiming controls to one-click profiles

summary.en: AMD's EXPO Ultra Low Latency program lets memory vendors include subtiming adjustments in validated profiles for the first time. The change gives desktop users a simpler route to lower latency, while shifting more tuning responsibility to module makers and their qualification process.

title.en: Equipment & Materials
OSI Systems wins a $10 million semiconductor-equipment order

summary.en: OSI Systems secured a semiconductor-equipment order valued at $10 million. The award is modest beside leading-edge fab tool budgets, but it provides a concrete demand signal for the specialized inspection and manufacturing equipment supplied below the largest tool vendors.

title.en: Soulbrain receives a $12 million state grant

summary.en: Soulbrain received a $12 million state grant connected to its semiconductor supply-chain operations. The award directs public funding toward a materials supplier, but the candidate report does not specify the production capacity or commissioning schedule that the grant will support.

title.en: Policy & Geopolitics
Nvidia's China chip sales move into Congress's sights

summary.en: US Senator Elizabeth Warren is pressing Nvidia chief Jensen Huang to testify about the company's chip sales to China. The scrutiny raises the political risk around future accelerator export licenses and could narrow Nvidia's room to design market-specific products for Chinese customers.

title.en: India and the Netherlands deepen semiconductor cooperation

summary.en: India and the Netherlands upgraded bilateral ties with a stronger semiconductor partnership. The pairing links India's push to build a domestic chip ecosystem with Dutch strengths in lithography and semiconductor equipment, although the report does not specify new fab or tool commitments.

title.en: Challengers & New Silicon
Ayar Labs deepens Nvidia and Wiwynn work on co-packaged optics

summary.en: Ayar Labs is expanding its work with Nvidia and server maker Wiwynn on co-packaged optical links for AI infrastructure. The relationships matter because electrical I/O power and reach are becoming system limits as accelerator clusters scale, though the report does not identify a production deployment.

theme.en: Long-Context AI Moves Into Systems Engineering

dek.en: This week's strongest papers treat attention routing, KV-cache layout, and persistent agent memory as systems problems rather than isolated model tricks. The supporting work extends from GPU arithmetic to multi-die FPGA routing and quantum rare-event sampling.

title.en: AI Accelerators & Compute-in-Memory
GPU implementation of multi-precision integer division

papers.authors: M. B. Marchioro, A. N. Raahauge, M. I. Lovenskjold, et al.

summary.en: This work maps Newton-iteration integer division to CUDA for precisions from 2^15 to 2^18 bits, beyond the division range supported by CGBN. Its integer-only, data-parallel formulation and multiplication-based cost model are relevant to cryptography and symbolic workloads on GPUs. The evidence is an implementation study rather than new silicon.

title.en: AI Research & Inference Systems
You Only Index Once: Cross-layer sparse attention with shared routing

papers.authors: Y. Sun, Y. Zhang, L. Dong, et al.

summary.en: Cross-Layer Sparse Attention computes token-level top-k routing once and reuses the index across decoder layers in KV-sharing architectures such as YOCO. That amortizes a cost that often erases the practical benefit of token-sparse attention while also reducing prefill, cache, and decode pressure. The result depends on architectures that already share KV state, so portability to conventional transformers remains the key caveat.

title.en: Vortex: Programmable sparse-attention serving for AI agents

papers.authors: Z. Chen, X. Zhong, Q. Feng, et al.

summary.en: Vortex combines a Python-embedded sparse-attention language with a page-centric tensor abstraction and a serving-stack backend. Algorithms generated and refined through the system reach up to 3.46 times the throughput of full attention while preserving accuracy in the reported tests. The preprint's main contribution is deployability and iteration speed, not a new attention rule.

title.en: Agent Memory: System implications of stateful long-horizon workloads

papers.authors: Y. Omri, Z. Gan, Z. Broveak, et al.

summary.en: This paper profiles ten agent-memory systems and attributes cost separately to memory construction, retrieval, and generation. The characterization shows that design choices move work between read and write paths, producing different scheduling, freshness, and fleet-management requirements. It is a systems taxonomy and benchmark study, so its value depends on how representative the selected frameworks and workloads are.

title.en: TokenMizer: Graph-structured memory for long LLM sessions

summary.en: TokenMizer stores session history as a typed knowledge graph and serializes it into compact resume blocks rather than carrying flat transcripts forward. On 21 controlled sessions, the blocks average 78 tokens versus 159 to 170 for the evaluated baselines while improving decision recall by 9 to 17 percentage points. The benchmark is small and controlled, so production-scale extraction cost and error accumulation remain open questions.

title.en: Tangram makes non-uniform KV caches practical for multi-turn serving

papers.authors: H. Kim, M. Kim, H. Kim, et al.

summary.en: Tangram assigns deterministic per-head cache budgets, groups heads with similar retention needs into vectorized page tables, and balances work ahead of time. The design targets fragmentation, scheduling overhead, and poor kernel utilization that can cancel the memory savings of non-uniform KV compression. It is a serving-system proposal, and the supplied abstract does not expose the full end-to-end benchmark results.

title.en: Quantum Computing
Quantum-enhanced rare-event discovery and sampling

papers.authors: N. Guo, P.-W. Huang, Q. Wang, et al.

summary.en: The algorithm discovers and samples events below a probability threshold without requiring the rare outcomes to be identified in advance. The authors derive optimal quantum scaling with the rarity threshold, including quadratic speedup for certain heavy-tailed systems and polynomial gains for stationary processes. These are theoretical complexity results; practical advantage will depend on state preparation and fault-tolerant execution costs.

title.en: EDA & Design Automation
Modeling and optimizing multi-die FPGA routing architectures

papers.authors: A. Poolad, S. G. Shahrouz, A. Boutros, et al.

summary.en: The work extends VTR and VPR to model, place, and route a wider range of 2.5D and 3D FPGA inter-die fabrics. HSPICE models cover active dice at 7 nm and a 45 nm silicon interposer, enabling design-space exploration across die-crossing technologies. The study improves open EDA support for multi-die FPGAs, but its conclusions remain simulation-based rather than measured on fabricated hardware.
